example: She is an ambitious young entrepreneur who wants to change the world.
hint: Pair it with goals or plans: 'ambitious goals', 'ambitious plan'.

example: They had to negotiate the terms of the contract for several weeks.
hint: Always between two sides: 'negotiate a deal', 'negotiate a salary'.

example: The company switched to sustainable packaging to reduce waste.
hint: Means long-term without harm: 'sustainable energy', 'sustainable growth'.

example: According to the forecast, it will rain all weekend.
hint: Works for weather, economy and sales: 'weather forecast', 'sales forecast'.

example: I am so grateful for all the help you gave me.
hint: Stronger than 'thankful'. Say: 'I'm grateful for...' or 'grateful to you for...'.

example: He dedicated his life to helping others in need.
hint: Always with 'to': 'dedicate time to', 'dedicate yourself to'.

example: Social media has a huge influence on modern culture.
hint: Use 'have influence on' — not 'make influence'.

example: After the long flight, she was completely exhausted.
hint: Much stronger than 'tired' — you're completely out of energy.

example: Please confirm your reservation at least 24 hours in advance.
hint: Formal and common in emails: 'confirm your booking', 'confirm receipt'.

example: My colleague helped me finish the report on time.
hint: More formal than 'co-worker' — use it in professional settings.

example: She accomplished all her goals by the end of the year.
hint: Implies effort + success: 'accomplish a goal', 'accomplish a task'.

example: Scientists made a major breakthrough in cancer research.
hint: Always 'make a breakthrough' — never 'do a breakthrough'.

example: He felt anxious before his job interview.
hint: 'Anxious about' = worried. Can also mean eager: 'anxious to help'.

example: We need to stick to our budget this month.
hint: Key phrases: 'stick to a budget', 'set a budget', 'exceed the budget'.

example: Try to look at the problem from a different perspective.
hint: 'From my perspective' — a polite way to share your opinion.

example: Teachers should encourage students to ask questions.
hint: Pattern: 'encourage someone to do something'.

example: Many people now work in remote positions from home.
hint: 'Remote work' and 'remote job' are standard in modern professional English.

example: It is wise to invest your savings rather than keep them in cash.
hint: Always 'invest in': 'invest in stocks', 'invest in yourself'.

example: You need a reliable car if you drive long distances every day.
hint: For people, machines and data. 'Reliable source' is common in academic writing.

example: The outcome of the meeting was better than expected.
hint: More formal than 'result' — focuses on the final effect of a process.

example: The old bridge collapsed after years of neglect.
hint: Literal (buildings) or figurative: a deal, economy, or relationship can collapse.

example: It takes time to adapt to a new country and culture.
hint: Always 'adapt to' — never 'adapt with'.

example: After six months she was offered a permanent position.
hint: Opposite of 'temporary'. Common in job ads: 'permanent position'.

example: There is a serious shortage of affordable housing in the city.
hint: Pattern: 'a shortage of something' — when supply is less than demand.

example: The demand for electric cars is growing every year.
hint: Noun: 'demand for'. Verb: 'demand that' — sounds firm and forceful.

example: Don't hesitate to call me if you need any help.
hint: 'Don't hesitate to...' = 'feel free to...' — very common in formal emails.

example: She made a conscious decision to eat more healthily.
hint: Don't confuse with 'conscience' — that's your moral sense.

example: The company plans to launch a new product next spring.
hint: For products, campaigns, websites — starting something new publicly.

example: Her smile was genuine, and everyone could feel her warmth.
hint: Opposite of fake. Also for objects: 'genuine leather', 'genuine signature'.

example: Every obstacle is an opportunity to learn something new.
hint: Common verbs: 'overcome', 'face', 'remove' an obstacle.
